title: Minimum Entropies of Braids
speaker: Chi Cheuk Tsang
abstract: Every braid can be thought of as a homeomorphism of a punctured disc. Morally, the more complicated a braid is, the more dynamics is contained in the corresponding homeomorphism, which one can quantify using topological entropy. In particular, one can easily construct braids with arbitrarily large entropies. The situation is more interesting at the other end of the spectrum, where one can ask for the minimum entropy of braids (with a fixed number of strands). In this talk, we present joint work with Xiangzhuo Zeng in answering this question.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Braids as mapping classes of punctured disks
proof_stages.summary: The talk models an n-strand braid as monotone strands in a cylinder whose upward sweep gives a homeomorphism, well defined up to isotopy, of a disk with n holes or punctures.

proof_stages.label: Topological entropy as braid complexity
proof_stages.summary: Topological entropy is defined via finite open covers, common refinements, and exponential growth of minimal covering numbers, and the entropy of a braid is the infimum over homeomorphisms in its isotopy class.

proof_stages.label: Zero entropy, positive entropy, and dilution mechanisms
proof_stages.summary: Basic computations show that identity and twist-type braids can have zero entropy, while the three-strand taffy/French braid has positive entropy computable through a torus lift, and Ferris-wheel constructions dilute entropy, motivating a fixed-strand minimum-positive-entropy problem.

proof_stages.label: Known minima and the new near-complete formula
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the minimum-entropy problem for n-strand braids, reviews known exact values and upper-bound families, and announces that the joint work determines the remaining values except for three unresolved cases, with Q&A discussing uniqueness, overlap with prior examples, scope, and the entropy spectrum.

proof_stages.label: Nielsen–Thurston reduction to pseudo-Anosov pieces
proof_stages.summary: Using the Nielsen–Thurston trichotomy, each surface homeomorphism is finite order, reducible, or pseudo-Anosov, and braid entropy reduces to the logarithm of the largest pseudo-Anosov stretch factor among the pieces.

proof_stages.label: Train-track matrices for stretch factors
proof_stages.summary: For pseudo-Anosov braids, invariant train tracks encode the limiting behavior of curves, and the spectral radius of the associated transition matrix recovers the dilatation, with nonuniqueness allowing smaller block forms.

proof_stages.label: Floral train tracks and lower bounds
proof_stages.summary: The new proof chooses floral train tracks with a central pistil, filaments, and petals for arbitrary braids, extracts structural constraints on their transition matrices, and converts these into spectral-radius lower bounds matching the constructed upper bounds.

examples.name: Identity braid and identity map
examples.note: The trivial braid gives the identity mapping class, serving as a sanity check that the open-cover definition of entropy gives zero.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs the entropy definition on the identity map, analyzes the pulled-back/refined covers, bounds their size independently of iteration, and concludes entropy zero.

passes.reason: The identity braid/map is the trivial zero-entropy boundary case used to make the naive all-braids minimum collapse to zero.
the no-crossing braid is the maximally regular identity with entropy 0, obstructing any positive lower bound over all braids
The identity braid/map is the trivial neutral object with maximal regularity and entropy 0 at the lower bound.
The identity braid/map is the trivial maximally regular zero-entropy lower-bound case used to collapse the unqualified minimum question.
the identity braid/map is the trivial neutral element with zero entropy, used to show the unrestricted minimum-entropy question collapses to zero
the no-crossing braid/identity homeomorphism is the trivial maximally invariant zero-entropy case that collapses the naive minimum problem to 0
The no-crossing braid is the trivial identity mapping class with minimal entropy zero and maximal self-symmetry, used as a baseline computation.
the identity braid/map is the trivial zero-entropy case used as an obstruction to the naive minimum-entropy question
the no-crossing identity is maximally regular, trivial, and zero-entropy minimal, used to collapse the naive minimization question
the trivial no-crossing braid/identity map has minimal zero entropy and is used to trivialize the naive minimization question
The identity braid/map is the trivial no-crossing, zero-entropy lower-end case used to show the naive minimum-entropy question collapses to zero.
the identity braid/map is the trivial zero-entropy neutral element, used as the basic minimum case rather than to refute a claim

examples.name: Two-strand annular twist braid
examples.note: The annular twist makes concrete how a braid records twisting in the mapping class, and its zero entropy despite nontriviality forces the move from “minimum entropy” to “minimum nonzero entropy.”
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws/reasons through the annular twist, tracks how an interval or open set wraps under iterates, estimates intersection growth as polynomial, and concludes the twist braid has entropy zero.

passes.reason: The two-strand annular twist is a reducible zero-entropy Dehn-twist case used to show nontrivial braids can still defeat a positive lower bound.
the annular Dehn twist is a reducible zero-entropy mapping class, used to show a nontrivial braid can still have no positive entropy
A two-strand annular twist is a reducible minimal nontrivial braid with entropy 0, in a standard twist family.
The two-strand annular Dehn-twist braid is a reducible zero-entropy boundary case showing that a nontrivial braid can still have no entropy.
the annular Dehn-twist braid is reducible and zero-entropy, obstructing the naive hope that a nontrivial braid has positive entropy
the two-strand annular twist is a reducible low-complexity Dehn-twist type braid with zero entropy, showing that nontriviality alone does not force positive entropy
The annular two-strand twist is a reducible zero-entropy braid, used to show that nontrivial braids can still have zero entropy.
the two-strand annular Dehn twist is reducible with zero entropy, showing a nontrivial braid still need not have positive entropy
the two-puncture annular Dehn twist is reducible and zero-entropy, used to show a nontrivial braid need not have positive entropy
the two-strand annular Dehn twist is a reducible zero-entropy nontrivial braid, blocking the hope that excluding only the identity gives positive entropy
The two-strand annular twist is a reducible Dehn-twist-type braid with zero entropy, obstructing any positive minimum after merely excluding the identity.
the two-strand annular Dehn twist is a nontrivial reducible braid with minimal entropy zero, obstructing a positive minimum over merely nontrivial braids

examples.name: Overlapping open cover for the identity
examples.note: An audience question about two overlapping open sets for the identity map clarifies that entropy counts minimal refined covers rather than all raw symbolic itineraries.
examples.engagement_reason: For the overlapping-cover objection to the identity case, the speaker explains why the apparent many itineraries do not give positive entropy and ties it to the finite refinement/least-cover-number computation.

passes.reason: The overlapping cover is an ordinary finite cover raised as an adversarial test of the naive itinerary-counting explanation for the identity.
a two-set overlapping cover is structurally ordinary, but it is posed as an apparent enemy case for the naive itinerary count
An ordinary overlapping finite cover is raised as an apparent adversarial case against the identity entropy computation.
The overlapping cover is structurally ordinary but is raised as an adversarial test of the naive itinerary-count argument for the identity.
an ordinary overlapping finite open cover is raised as an apparent enemy case to the naive itinerary-counting interpretation for the identity
the overlapping cover is an ordinary finite open cover raised as an apparent objection to the identity map having zero entropy
An overlapping finite open cover is structurally ordinary, but it is posed as an adversarial case against the naive itinerary-count interpretation for the identity.
the overlapping cover is raised as an adversarial apparent counterexample to entropy zero for the identity, but it is an ordinary finite open cover
the overlapping finite cover is an ordinary cover deployed as an adversarial test of the identity-entropy computation
the overlapping cover is an ordinary cover used as a would-be challenge to the claim that the identity has zero entropy
The overlapping cover is an ordinary finite open cover raised as an adversarial case against the naive itinerary-counting explanation for the identity map.
the overlapping open cover is an ordinary cover raised as an adversarial case that seems to give exponentially many itineraries for the identity

examples.name: Four-strand multitwist variant
examples.note: In response to a question, a braid twisting around disjoint curves illustrates that reducible multitwist-type braids also have zero entropy.
examples.engagement_reason: The four-strand multitwist is only presented as another twist-along-curves variant to which the same zero-entropy idea would apply, without a concrete computation or construction being carried out on it.

passes.reason: The four-strand multitwist is a reducible zero-entropy multitwist preserving curves, extending the obstruction to positive entropy.
the four-strand multitwist is a reducible abelian zero-entropy braid, used to defeat positivity for broader nontrivial braids
The four-strand example is a standard reducible multitwist on disjoint curves with zero entropy at the lower boundary.
The four-strand multitwist is a reducible zero-entropy lower-bound case extending the obstruction from single twists.
the four-strand multitwist is a reducible product of twists with zero entropy, again showing nontrivial/reducible braids need not have positive entropy
the four-strand multitwist is reducible and zero-entropy, serving as another obstruction to any positive lower bound for merely nontrivial braids
The four-strand product of twists along disjoint curves is reducible and zero-entropy, extending the obstruction to positive entropy beyond the simple twist case.
the four-strand multitwist is a reducible zero-entropy braid, extending the obstruction from simple twists to multitwists
the four-strand multitwist is reducible and zero-entropy, used as another obstruction to getting positive entropy from nontrivial braiding
the four-strand multitwist is a reducible multitwist with zero entropy, giving another nontrivial obstruction to a positive lower bound
The four-strand multitwist is a reducible zero-entropy braid, showing that even more nontrivial multitwist examples do not give positive entropy.
the four-strand multitwist is another reducible zero-entropy braid, showing that more complicated-looking nontrivial multitwists still need not have positive entropy

examples.name: Closed-surface comparison cases
examples.note: The torus and genus-two cases are mentioned in Q&A to situate how little is known about analogous minimum-entropy problems beyond punctured disks.
examples.engagement_reason: Closed-surface cases are only cited in comparison—known for genus one and genus two, with hyperelliptic comments—without working through any specific closed-surface example.

passes.reason: The closed-surface and hyperelliptic comparison cases are minimum-entropy benchmark cases, with hyperelliptic symmetry, rather than refutations.
